commands.repair_reason: Manual review is needed because this external security PR has a concrete auth-race finding and still needs contributor-owned real WebSocket proof before merge.; Needs attention: Needs attention because the security hardening can still be bypassed across the async dispatch boundary; no new supply-chain surface was found.; Review finding: [P2] Recheck invalidation at final dispatch — src/gateway/server/ws-connection/m... (sha=779f657ed02e71c92791e7e6da364b06fc67b9fe)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds synchronous gateway client invalidation for device-token rotation, revocation, and pair removal paths, plus a WebSocket dispatch guard and focused tests.

Reproducibility: yes. source-level: current main responds before the queueMicrotask-scheduled device disconnect and then dispatches later requests without any device-token invalidation check. I did not run a live pipelined WebSocket repro.
commands.review_followup: Manual review is needed because this external security PR has a concrete auth-race finding and still needs contributor-owned real WebSocket proof before merge.

target.title: fix(gateway): reject RPCs from invalidated device-token clients durin…
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `device.token.rotate`, `device.token.revoke`, and `device.pair.remove` respond 200 OK then defer `disconnectClientsForDevice` via `queueMicrotask`. RPCs already pipelined in the WS buffer land with the rotated/revoked token before the socket closes.
- **Why it matters:** An attacker can execute authenticated operations with a token that should already be invalidated — privilege persistence after revocation.
- **What changed:** Added a synchronous `invalidated` flag on `GatewayWsClient`, set before `respond()` in all three handlers. Added a per-RPC dispatch guard that force-closes flagged clients. Introduced `context.invalidateClientsForDevice()` as a sync companion to the existing async disconnect.
- **What did NOT change:** Shared-auth re-check path (already guarded at `message-handler.ts:1444-1458`), disconnect scheduling (still `queueMicrotask`), admin response flushing behavior.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Auth / tokens

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #71156 (documents the race in AGENTS.md as "open/in-flight")
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** `queueMicrotask` deferral of socket close created a window where the rotated/revoked token was still accepted. No per-RPC re-check existed for device-token auth.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No synchronous invalidation mechanism; no test covering pipelined RPCs during the microtask window.
- **Contributing context:** The shared-auth path already had per-RPC re-check; device-token auth was assumed to be covered by socket close, but socket close is async.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- **Scenario:** Pipelined RPCs after `invalidateClientsForDevice()` are rejected; `invalidated` flag is set before `respond()` returns.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

RPCs arriving on a device-token WS connection after token rotation/revocation are now rejected immediately instead of being processed until socket close completes.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
[rotate/revoke] -> [respond 200] -> [queueMicrotask: disconnect] -> [pipelined RPCs execute ✗]

After:
[rotate/revoke] -> [set invalidated flag] -> [respond 200] -> [pipelined RPCs hit dispatch guard → force-close] -> [queueMicrotask: disconnect]
```

## Security Impact

- Secrets/tokens handling changed? Yes
- **Risk + mitigation:** The `invalidated` flag is a new in-memory gate on RPC dispatch. Risk is false-positive invalidation; mitigated by setting the flag only in the three explicit revocation handlers.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `device.token.rotate`, `device.token.revoke`, and `device.pair.remove` responded 200 OK then deferred `disconnectClientsForDevice` via `queueMicrotask`. RPCs already pipelined in the WS buffer executed with the revoked token before the socket closed — privilege persistence after revocation.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 25.4.0 / Node 23.7.0, project source checkout, `node --import tsx/esm` run directly against the call-chain ordering pattern.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
node --import tsx/esm proof-70707.mts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

Before the fix (invalidation deferred via queueMicrotask):
```
=== BEFORE FIX (invalidation deferred via queueMicrotask) ===
  [rotate] respond(200 OK)
  [microtask] invalidation fires — but RPCs already ran
  Pipelined RPCs executed with revoked token: [sessions.list, agents.run]
  → Privilege persistence after revocation!
```

After the fix (invalidated flag set synchronously before respond):
```
=== AFTER FIX (invalidated flag set synchronously before respond) ===
  [rotate] invalidated flag SET synchronously
  [rotate] respond(200 OK)
  [microtask] socket close fires — but RPCs already blocked
  Pipelined RPCs executed: none ✓
  Rejected by dispatch guard: [sessions.list, agents.run] ✓
  → No privilege persistence after revocation.
```

**Observed result after fix:** Pipelined RPCs are rejected immediately by the dispatch guard. The synchronous `invalidated` flag closes the microtask window before any buffered RPC can execute.

**What was not tested:** Full integration test with real pipelined WebSocket traffic under load. The `node` script above isolates the exact ordering pattern and produces real before/after output demonstrating the race and its fix.
target.author: davidangularme
